topic: Understanding compounding transformations through iterative loops to create complex geometric patterns.

concepts.name: compounding rotation
concepts.explanation: Instead of setting an absolute angle, you add a small amount to the current rotation within a loop. This means each subsequent shape inherits the cumulative rotation of all previous shapes in that frame.
concepts.why_it_matters: It allows you to create complex, organic-looking patterns and 'illusory' motion where objects appear to rotate at different speeds.

concepts.name: stateful transformations
concepts.explanation: The instructor explains that transformations are cumulative within a single draw cycle; the coordinate system remembers the rotation from the previous loop iteration.
concepts.why_it_matters: Understanding that transformations are additive is key to controlling how complex patterns emerge from simple code.

concepts.name: visual scaffolding
concepts.explanation: Using the loop index `i` to scale shapes (e.g., `rect(..., i * 10)`) so that the mathematical changes in rotation are visually distinguishable.
concepts.why_it_matters: If all shapes look identical, you can't see if your math is actually working.

code_examples.demonstrates: Drawing multiple rectangles that increase in size based on the loop index.
code_examples.common_issue: If you don't change the size or position based on `i`, all shapes will be drawn exactly on top of each other, making it look like nothing happened.

code_examples.demonstrates: Compounding rotation where each rectangle is rotated slightly more than the one before it.
code_examples.common_issue: If `rotate()` is called outside the loop, all shapes will rotate by the same amount and look identical.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: Everything on the screen is rotating together.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: The `rotate()` function is being applied to the global coordinate system without any way to reset it.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Use `push()` and `pop()` (to be covered in the next video) to isolate transformations.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The shapes are all overlapping and look like a messy blob.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Using `fill()` instead of `noFill()` when drawing multiple overlapping shapes.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Use `noFill()` to see the outlines of all shapes in the loop.

key_moments.description: The instructor points out the limitation of global rotation: you can't easily make different objects rotate at different speeds if they all share the same global rotation.
Moving the `rotate()` function inside the loop to show how rotation becomes cumulative/compounded.
The instructor identifies the 'failure' of this method (everything still rotates) to introduce the need for `push()` and `pop()`.

prerequisites: Basic use of the rotate() function
leads_to: push() and pop() for local transformations
student_questions: Why are all my shapes rotating together?
How do I make a pattern of things that rotate at different speeds?
Why does my code only show one shape even though I'm using a loop?
How do I make shapes get bigger as they rotate?
My shapes are all overlapping and I can't see the individual ones, how do I fix that?
How do I make a shape rotate slightly more than the one before it in a loop?
Why is my rotation increasing every time the loop runs?
